expectations.text: Applies the generics restraint principle from the skill
expectations.evidence: 'The generic `Repository[T]` base provides minimal value here.' — directly applies generics restraint.

expectations.text: Notes that each subclass adds domain-specific methods the generic base doesn't capture
expectations.evidence: 'Both `UserRepository` and `OrderRepository` immediately add domain-specific methods — the generic base captures only a trivial skeleton (`get`, `save`, `delete`) that every repository would have anyway.'

expectations.text: Questions whether the generic base justifies the abstraction overhead
expectations.evidence: 'The generic base doesn't enable polymorphism — nowhere in the codebase will you write `def do_something(repo: Repository[T])`'

expectations.text: Suggests a caller-facing Protocol as an alternative or complement
expectations.evidence: 'A caller-facing `Protocol` would provide more value if you genuinely need to swap implementations.'

expectations.text: Does not simply endorse the generic design without evaluating whether it adds value
expectations.evidence: Recommends removing the generic base and using plain concrete repositories instead.

expectations.text: Recommends Protocol for the caller-facing contract

expectations.text: Explains that structural typing means plugins don't need to inherit
expectations.evidence: 'Protocol uses structural typing — any class with matching methods satisfies the contract without inheriting from your base. Plugin authors don't need to `import` your interface.'

expectations.text: Distinguishes between the caller-facing contract (Protocol) and implementor convenience (ABC)
expectations.evidence: 'Use `ABC` only if you need to provide shared implementation that plugin authors inherit... Even then, callers should type against the `Protocol`, not the `ABC`.'

expectations.text: Notes that callers should always type against the Protocol, not the ABC
